Figure 6: Implementation of dual verification tree.

Figure 1: Performance analysis of π-0.5 under varying execution horizons on LIBERO benchmark [8]. (a) Success rate first increases while then decreases as horizon increases, dropping below 80% when horizon is larger than 15 for most suites. (b) Completion step increases substantially with a larger horizon, as failed recoveries and compounding errors drive total steps up to 1.5× higher than at horizon=1. (c) Forward calls decrease monotonically with a longer horizon, revealing a fundamental trade-off between inference overhead and success rate.
Table 1: Dynamic horizon comparison across LIBERO, MetaWorld, and MainSkill. We report average task success rate (Avg.) and average committed horizon length (Len.).
Table 2: Performance comparison on real-world tasks.
Figure 3: Trade-off between success rate (top row) and forward calls (bottom row) across execution horizons. The first two column experiments are conducted on π-0.5 while the last one is on GR00T.

Figure 2: Overview of A3. Given the current observation and instruction, the VLM backbone and action expert generate K candidate action chunks. The chunks are mapped to induced trajectory states, from which the dominant mode is identified via clustering and its medoid selected as the primary draft; per-step consensus scores reflect the model’s self-consistency at each action position. The draft then undergoes dual parallel verification in a single batched forward pass, yielding the execution horizon l∗ as the longest verified prefix. We also illustrate the implementation of dual verification tree in Figure 6.
Figure 5: Two representative failure cases. (a) Misalignment between the mug handle and the hook in the hang mug task. (b) Self-occlusion of the inverted mug rim by the gripper in the flip mug task.
Table 4: Effect of prediction horizon on execution horizon and LIBERO suites.
